Complete Column D for every row. For rows asking for years of experience, enter the number of years and include a brief note on scope or relevant clients. For all other rows, provide a written response. Specific, evidence-based answers are expected.

Complete Column D for every row. For rows asking for years of experience, enter the number of years and include a brief note on scope or relevant clients. For all other rows, provide a written response. Specific, evidence-based answers are expected.
Track Record — Financial Services Issuer Services

Describe your organization's overall track record serving card-issuing financial institutions. Include years in market, number of active issuer clients, approximate ACV managed, and any notable performance milestones.

Genpact delivers end-to-end Customer Experience services to global clients across Banking & Financial Services, Fintech & Hi-Tech, Manufacturing, Life Sciences, Healthcare, CPG, and Insurance. We manage $130Bn+ in retail lending assets and support 600+ processes across the retail banking value chain, including Omni-Channel Customer Service, Contact Centre Analytics & Dashboards, Account Maintenance, Query & Dispute Management, Fraud, Payments, Billing and Refunds, Chargeback Processing, Ongoing AML Screening and Transaction Monitoring, KYC Refresh/EDD for High-Risk customers, and Service to Sales. Our operations span Omni-Channel Operations, Transformation Services, and Customer Experience Analytics, with proven delivery across prepaid, HCCA, and fraud authentication and verification services. Globally, we drive large-scale transformation for Tier 1 banks — achieving a 50%+ productivity improvement in the first four years of a five-year strategic partnership across Personal, Commercial, Business Banking, Mortgages, Payments, Collections, and Financial Crime. Our global footprint combines consulting, digital, technology, analytics, and intelligent operations, leveraging agentic AI, advanced ML models, GenAI, and scalable cloud infrastructure to deliver operational efficiency, enhanced customer experience, and digital modernization across industries, domains, and geographies. Notable performance milestones  include an Industry leading NPS score of 84 and $300+ million impact delivered.

Describe your approach to managing sub-contractors used in delivering TMS services. Confirm: (1) whether sub-contractors would be used on this engagement, which subcontractors would be used and where work would be performed; (2) your SLA flow-down mechanism; (3) whether the client retains audit rights over sub-contractors.
No subcontractors shall be engaged in connection with this engagement except for Genpact affiliates, which shall be identified and set forth in the applicable Statement of Work.

How many years has your organization delivered end-to-end risk mitigation covering fraud, collections, disputes, and credit bureau management. Report the number of years your organization has continuously operated this function. Do not sum individual staff years across people — report the longest unbroken period your organization has actively delivered this service.

Genpact has been providing fraud prevention and dispute  operations solutions to various banking and capital markets clients over the last 25+ years . The solutions provided by Genpact cover the entire end to end fraud prevention lifecycle including fraud related inbound/outbound calls/chats (includes customer verification, transaction review, case intake, account actions) , detection alert review, alert investigation, and account actions.

Our dispute operations front operations capabilities include dispute intake through various channels including calls, email, and chat channels. This includes identifying the dispute category and creating cases for back-office operations.  In back-office operations we support dispute investigation, chargeback eligibility checks,  chargeback assessment,  documents review,  chargeback management from 1st chargeback to arbitration support for our clients. Our fraud recovery teams are experienced in reviewing and validating cases and managing recoveries. 
 We are a team of 10,000 + fraud and dispute management professionals, operating out of 10 centers across the globe.

COLLECTIONS:
Genpact brings over 25+ years of global collections experience, delivering measurable outcomes through digital, data, and AI‑led operations across 30+ countries and 52+ languages. Genpact supports end‑to‑end collections covering customer engagement, hardship handling, compliance, disputes, analytics, and escalations. 
Proven benchmarks include up to 20–30% efficiency gains, 15% improvement in cash collected, 5% reduction in complaints and  35-40% Self serve/ Containment. Genpact combines deep domain expertise, scalable cloud platforms, and agentic AI to optimize recovery, compliance, and customer experience.

How many years has your organization delivered customer care for card programs (commercial, consumer credit, debit, retail, loyalty, healthcare, prepaid). Scope includes: IVR and live agent handling, multilingual and after-hours support, lost/stolen servicing, and loyalty programs. Report the number of years your organization has continuously operated this function. Do not sum individual staff years across people — report the longest unbroken period your organization has actively delivered this service.

Genpact began doing customer care as a stand alone company on 2005 but before that was doing this work as part of GE since 1997.  We have relationships with financial services companies where we have been doing customer care work that have existied for over 17 consecutive years.  Genpact services all types of card products, credit, debit, pre-paid, BNPL, co-branded and retail.  Our global footprint allows us to provide 24/7/365 servicing across all channels, voice, chat, email, secured messaging and social media.  Our team proivide service across over 50 languages and we maintain best in class language translation technology that allows us to service various languages from anywhere in the world.

How many years has your organization delivered back-office processing for card programs. Scope includes: imaging, correspondence processing, payment research, balance transfers, credit balance refunds, account maintenance, general ledger (GL) balancing and settlement, reconciliation of GL accounts, and returned mail processing. Note: application processing is not in scope for this engagement. Report the number of years your organization has continuously operated this function. Do not sum individual staff years across people — report the longest unbroken period your organization has actively delivered this service.

Genpact has been delivering large scale back office processing for card programs for 25+ years, supporting Tier 1 global banks, card issuers and fintech’s across highly regulated environments.

Our end to end operations capabilities span onboarding & issuance, servicing & maintenance, payment processing, settlements, and reconciliations, underpinned by strong controls and compliance. Our back office capabilities cover the full breadth of card program support, including imaging and document digitization, correspondence processing, exception handling, account maintenance, GL processing and reconciliations, returned mail handling, and compliance driven reporting, delivered across hundreds of processes supporting billions in assets and transactions globally. 

We combine deep domain expertise with Agentic AI, GenAI enabled solutions, predictive models, and advanced workflow orchestration to drive straight through processing, reduce manual effort, and improve accuracy. This integrated model has delivered higher STP, significant AHT reduction, lower rework, and improved reconciliation auto match rates, enabling scalable, resilient, and cost efficient card operations.
Years of Experience — Client Operations Oversight

How many years has your organization delivered structured client-facing operational support. Scope includes: day-to-day client inquiry and escalation management, monthly and quarterly business reviews, and SLA monitoring. Report the number of years your organization has continuously operated this function. Do not sum individual staff years across people — report the longest unbroken period your organization has actively delivered this service.

We have 29 years of experience. Our organization has continuously delivered structured, client‑facing operational support since 1997, beginning as a captive unit to GE Capital (GECIS), where we supported GE businesses and their end clients across multiple service lines. This model required day‑to‑day inquiry handling and escalation management, formal SLA tracking, and structured monthly and quarterly operational reviews with business stakeholders. Following our spin‑off from GE in 2005, these same capabilities have been delivered without interruption to external clients globally.
Number of Active Financial Services Clients
How many active financial services clients does your organization currently serve? Break out by client type (issuer, acquirer, payments processor, other) where possible.

300+ clients, we deliver the full spectrum of services. Genpact offers a range of products and services for digital payments, customer service, fraud, consumer and treasury management payments, cash applications, invoice to cash, lockbox/checks and ecommerce services, but does not operate a payments platform.  We bring expertise in experience design,  AI, data and technology to build new payments products, manage technology services and transform and run  operations.

India - Company Owned

Poland - Company Owned
Guatemala - Company owned

Which languages do you currently support across your delivery network? List associated delivery locations. Of particular relevance: English (US, CA, UK), Spanish (US), French-Canadian, German, Dutch, Portuguese.

English: United States (Atlanta; Wilkes-Barre; Richardson; Bloomington; Jacksonville; Danville; Chicago; Portland; Bentonville), Canada (Mississauga), United Kingdom (Belfast; Manchester).

Spanish: United States (Atlanta; Wilkes-Barre; Richardson; Bloomington; Jacksonville; Danville; Chicago; Portland; Bentonville), United Kingdom (Manchester), Romania (Bucharest; Cluj), Poland (Lublin; Krakow; Katowice), Hungary (Budapest), Germany (Munich), Mexico (Juarez; Guadalajara), Costa Rica (Heredia), Guatemala (Guatemala City).

French-Canadian: Canada (Mississauga).

German: United Kingdom (Manchester), Romania (Bucharest; Cluj), Poland (Lublin; Krakow; Katowice), Hungary (Budapest), Germany (Munich), Egypt (Cairo).

Dutch: United Kingdom (Manchester), Romania (Bucharest; Cluj), Poland (Krakow), Netherlands (Hoofddorp), Hungary (Budapest).

Portuguese: China (Shanghai), Romania (Bucharest; Cluj), Poland (Lublin), Mexico (Monterrey), Brazil (Uberlandia).

What is your total headcount across all geographies? Break out by: (1) front-office agent FTE; (2) back-office agent FTE; (3) support FTE (QA, WFM, Training, Management, Analytics); (4) approximate onshore / nearshore / offshore split (%).

Our global delivery model is supported by a scalable workforce with strong presence across customer operations and collections. Genpact has over 140,000 employees serving various geographies and customers.

Breakdown by function:
•	Front-office agent FTE : ~65–70%
•	Back-office agent FTE and Support FTE : 30-35%
Geographic delivery mix:
•	Offshore: ~85%
•	Nearshore: ~10%
•	Onshore: ~5%
This operating model enables us to deliver cost-efficient, scalable, and high-quality services while maintaining strong operational governance, domain expertise, and 24/7 coverage aligned to client requirements.
What were your voluntary agent attrition rates for each of the last three calendar years? Break out by geography where materially different.

We have maintained voluntary attrition within competitive industry benchmarks, with a clear downward trend over the past three years driven by focused investments in employee engagement, career development, and frontline leadership effectiveness.

Our overall voluntary attrition has improved from approximately 24–26% in 2023, to 20–22% in 2025, reflecting the impact of targeted retention initiatives, structured learning pathways, and enhanced employee experience programs.

What is your average agent tenure overall? Break out by primary function where available: Customer Service, Fraud Operations, Disputes & Chargebacks, Debt & Collections, Back Office, and Risk & Compliance.

Our workforce demonstrates a balanced mix of stability and agility, supported by structured training programs, clear career progression pathways, and ongoing employee engagement initiatives. Overall average agent tenure: ~2.5–3 years
Optional. Are there any industry-relevant certifications, training frameworks, or accreditations held by your workforce that are applicable to financial services contact center operations?

Our workforce is enabled through a strong blend of industry-recognized certifications such as CFE and ACAMS, structured training frameworks, and role-based accreditations aligned to fraud and dispute operations.

We have institutionalized talent development through our flagship FCRM Talent Academy. This program partners with academic institutions to scout early talent and combines deep SME-led training with curated content and real-world case studies developed alongside our training partners.

Our investment in external certifications is further strengthened by robust internal accreditations across fraud, disputes, and quality roles—ensuring strong customer empathy in the front office and rigorous investigation and control discipline in the back office.

Demonstrate the expected improvement in SLAs/KPIs over time, from transition through steady state. Proposed commitments may be incorporated into contractual service level schedules.
Vendor to provide approach to improving SLA / KPI

Combine RTA, routing optimization, break staggering, and call blending during peaks

Align schedules to interval level demand, prioritize high SLA queues, overflow tuning logic

Improve interval coverage, prioritize queues, reduce wait time via better distribution

Knowledge base improvement, call flow simplification, agent coaching

Reduce wait time via routing + callback option + peak handling

Phase 1: Baseline Assessment (0–6 Months)

Post Go-Live Baselining: Each transitioned wave will be baselined for volume, AHT, SLAs and KPIs over ~6 months.

Establish a robust performance baseline by analyzing actual AHT, SL, ASA, and demand patterns at interval level.
Identify gaps between planned vs. actual capacity, adherence, and queue performance to understand true on-ground dynamics.

Phase 2: Opportunity Identification & Strategy Design (2–3 Months)

Evaluate key improvement levers across scheduling, RTA, routing, and process efficiencies.
Prioritize high-impact interventions and define a structured action plan aligned to SLA targets and operational constraints.

Phase 3: Implementation & Controlled Optimization (Ongoing)

Execute identified initiatives in a phased manner with continuous monitoring against baseline metrics.
Stabilize performance through controlled rollout, real-time governance, and iterative refinements to ensure sustained SLA improvement.

This tab covers four areas: (1) technology capability mapping — current TSYS applications and expected future-state are pre-populated for reference; respond with your proposed solution and migration approach; (2) technology governance commitments; (3) AI overlay capabilities; (4) Technology Costs You Are Willing to Fund and (5) a Technology responsibility Matrix between yourself and FIS/TSYS;
The migration approach and target architecture columns are intentionally open. FIS is not prescribing a migration methodology or architecture pattern. Vendors should propose the approach they would actually use based on the current and future-state stack shown. Responses that reflect a genuine assessment of the TSYS-to-FIS technology context will be evaluated more favorably than generic migration frameworks.

Can any technologies be decommissioned/rationalized as a result of this AI overlay? Please identify the impacted applications.
Voice Agents IVA ( End to End front to back) office​

Multi-agent system (Agents 1–3) handling inbound fraud call intake via Whisper/IVR stream: context assembly from Falcon, transaction validation via CH dialogue, claim creation in DRC, card block/unblock, and NICE call summary. Human-in-the-loop for high-risk whisper flags and edge cases.
~50–55% net FTE reduction on FAM FO agent-handled calls; IVR self-containment 13%→25%+; eliminates K6 scripting inconsistency and K10 manual NICE logging
API (MCP preferred); computer-use fallback if Falcon API not contracted
Genpact AI Platform; Claude (Anthropic); Falcon MCP; DRC MCP; NICE; Whisper/Voice AI; HITL Review UI

Falcon MCP API contract is critical (no contract = computer-use fallback, STP ceiling drops ~15–20 pts); Whisper STT pipeline custom build; DRC write API required for claim creation; ~107K alerts/month volume baseline

Text-based agentic interface for cardholder dispute intake and status inquiries — mirrors Voice Agent logic but via digital channel. Reduces inbound call volume and supports asynchronous dispute handling for T4/T5/T6 case types.
Incremental contact deflection on top of IVA; estimated 10–15% reduction in live-agent chat volume (to be validated with FIS volume data)
Genpact AI Platform; Claude (Anthropic); DRC MCP; HITL Review UI
Dependent on DRC API contract (Phase 0 gate); requires FIS digital channel integration (web/app SDK); volume baseline needed for sizing

Real-time language translation layer integrated into voice and chat agent flows — enables multilingual cardholder support without dedicated bilingual FTE pools. Translates CH input → English for agent processing, and agent output → CH language for delivery.
Eliminates ~100% of premium bilingual staffing costs for supported languages; estimated 5–8% of call volume currently requiring translation support (to confirm with FIS)
Genpact AI Platform; Claude (Anthropic); Whisper STT; Voice AI pipeline
Language coverage scope TBD with FIS; accuracy thresholds for regulated call types (Reg Z) must be validated; GDPR/TCPA compliance review required for recorded multilingual calls

Real-time AI co-pilot surfaced on analyst/agent desktop during live calls or case work. Provides next-best-action prompts, auto-populated data from Falcon/DRC/Mainframe, decision rule guidance, and draft response text — reduces cognitive load and AHT without full automation.
15–25% AHT reduction on assisted calls; quality improvement on HITL-reviewed cases; bridges gap before full straight-through automation is achievable
Genpact AI Platform; Claude (Anthropic); Falcon MCP; DRC MCP; HITL Review UI
Requires HITL UI build (shared dependency with agentic stack); Falcon read API sufficient (write not required for assist mode); analyst desktop integration (CRM/DRC screen) scoping needed

Automated quality assurance scoring of 100% of calls and case decisions vs. human sampling (~5–10% today). Evaluates Reg Z / FCBA / UDAAP compliance, script adherence, HITL threshold calibration accuracy, and decision logic consistency. Feeds findings into CAI flywheel.
100% call/case QA coverage vs. ~5–10% human sampling; estimated 60–70% reduction in manual QA FTE; compliance risk reduction through systematic Reg Z/FCBA audit trail

We do not seek to be compensated for the loss of profit or margin. Our termination fees are designed to mitigate costs that cannot be recovered. These termination charges typically include:
1. Termination fee for employee costs such as severance/redeployment of displaced colleagues
2. Unamortized/deferred charges beyond completion of service
3. Any deal investments
4. Any third party breakage fees and lease termination penalties
Notice period of 120 days to be provided in case of termination event

A de-identified client dataset will be provided alongside this RFP. A migration wave is a defined group of clients that will transition to the new operating model together, with a shared go-live date and dual-run period. (1) Using the supplemental data provided, please architect waves of clients/queues/locations/ regions and articulate the rationale. (2) Use the Wave Summary below to plot your wave against time. (3) Define a RACI for the Client Migration efforts.

Part 1 — Wave Composition: For each wave, list which client cohorts are included, explaining what criteria drove the grouping; for all clients, specify which client is mapped to each wave.  FIS has intentionally not prescribed a wave structure or grouping methodology. We expect vendors to analyze the client dataset provided and propose the wave design they believe is most operationally sound for an engagement of this complexity. There is no correct number of waves. Responses that demonstrate genuine analytical thinking about client risk, regulatory complexity, volume sequencing, and transition readiness will be evaluated more favorably than generic frameworks.

Phased and Risk-Balanced Approach: By sequencing NA (Columbus, Canada, PHP) first, we adopt a phased execution strategy - 
•	Lower Regulatory and Complexity Risks, compared to Europe
•	Stabilize high-confidence regions early
•	Incorporate learnings and best practices from similar previous engagements
•	Genpact’s approach in Canada would be to rehire employees following a clear and compliant separation from FIS. 
•	This structured rehire model ensures full adherence to local labor and employment regulations while enabling continuity of services, retention of critical skills, and minimal disruption to ongoing operations
•	PHP - Rebadging in the Philippines ensures uninterrupted delivery by retaining experienced, client‑embedded talent in a proven, cost‑efficient location already supporting Gamma 7 operations

The UK is partially aligned through Wave 1, targeting segments with comparable readiness and risk profiles.
•	UK Wave 1 includes roles with minimal regulatory dependency and no requirement for extended consultation, allowing alignment with the NA timeline and early stabilization of critical operations.

Risk: Change communication and logistics  - Delayed/Inadequate communication due to multi country engagement
Mitigation:  
•	Drive region‑specific communication aligned with the Transition Plan, supported by a detailed change communication roadmap initiated 
•	Open lines of communication – multi level governance

Risk - Attrition and knowledge loss - Low offer acceptance rate / High rate of Offer drops 
Mitigation - 
•	Ring fence key resources early – use retention tools 
•	Proactive contingency hiring to manage any gaps

Remaining UK (50%) deferred to Wave 2, which provides a longer runway to address regulatory and complexity‑led dependencies before cutover, reducing execution and compliance risk
•	UK Wave 2 may include roles subject to formal consultation requirements, increased regulatory dependency, or additional change‑management readiness.
•	Sequencing these roles in Wave 2 allows sufficient time to complete consultation processes and address employee feedback
•	Execution of Wave 2 will incorporate learnings from NA and UK Wave 1, enabling a more informed, compliant, and lower‑risk transition while maintaining business continuity.

Risk: Consultation & Regulatory Delays - Extended employee consultation or regulatory requirements may delay rebadging timelines.
Mitigation:
•	Plan consultations early with defined milestones and owners.
•	Partner with local HR and Legal to meet all statutory obligations.
•	Reuse proven materials and playbooks from NA and UK Wave 1.

Risk: Employee Uncertainty & Attrition (Wave 2) - The execution of rebadging in Wave 1, combined with a time gap of 2 months before Wave 2, may create uncertainty and perceived inequity among remaining employees, reducing engagement and increasing the risk of attrition in critical roles.
Mitigation:
•	Deploy a focused change management and communications plan for Wave 2.
•	Provide clear, regular updates on timing, rationale, and next steps.
•	Identify critical roles and implement targeted retention or engagement actions.

Risk: Operational Disruption During Rebadging
Wave 2 roles may be operationally critical, increasing the risk of service disruption if transitions are poorly sequenced or executed.
Mitigation:
•	Complete role level criticality and dependency assessments ahead of execution.
•	Establish robust transition and knowledge transfer plans.
•	Sequence execution to avoid peak operational demand conflicts.

EMEA is intentionally sequenced as the final wave due to its multi‑country regulatory landscape, varying labor laws, and extended consultation requirements. 
Positioning Europe after NA and UK ensures the rebadging is executed using a fully tested approach, minimizing legal exposure, operational disruption, and employee‑related risks.
•	Enter Europe with a proven, lower‑risk rebadging model

Risk: Regulatory and compliance gaps due to diverse labor laws, employment regulations, and consultation requirements across multiple European countries.
Mitigation:  Country-Specific Regulatory Assessment - Engage local legal expertise to validate regulatory interpretations and ensure alignment with jurisdiction‑specific requirements.
Identify countries requiring works council or employee representative consultation early in the planning phase.
Ensure documentation, contracting, and onboarding fully align with local employment regulations.

Part 2 — Wave Timeline: For each wave, state the proposed start date, go-live date, and dual-run duration. Please visually differentiate between the migration and dual run periods, if applicable. FIS has intentionally not prescribed a wave structure or grouping methodology. We expect vendors to analyze the client dataset provided and propose the wave design they believe is most operationally sound for an engagement of this complexity. There is no correct number of waves. Responses that demonstrate genuine analytical thinking about client risk, regulatory complexity, volume sequencing, and transition readiness will be evaluated more favorably than generic frameworks.
Wave 1 (Columbus, Canada, PHP and Milton Keynes)

Genpact applies a global approach in ensuring compliance with specific data protection laws, including for example EU, US (various states), Canada, Australia, Philippines, Brazil, South Africa, China. Genpact Global Legal & Compliance team identifies laws and regulations that are applicable to Genpact businesses, functions and operational teams across the relevant regions. Genpact assesses and complies with laws applicable to it in the operation and management of its business. With respect to our clients’ operations, Genpact relies on the client identifying laws and regulations that apply to the client and the specific services to be performed by Genpact. Any operational requirements necessary for the operations to comply with applicable laws are written into the standard operating procedures that are approved by the client, and Genpact then performs services to the standards and in the manner set out in the standard operating procedures.
PCI DSS — confirm current certification level, scope, and most recent assessment date.

Genpact does not undergo PCI DSS certification at an enterprise level.

PCI certification is maintained for specific business accounts/clients where Genpact is exposed to card data and is contractually required to store, process, and transmit credit card data in compliance with PCI DSS requirements, including for specific processes conducted at clients’ request and expense.

For PCI DSS certified locations, the certification scope includes physical, network, and logical controls aligned to PCI DSS v4. 0 standards.
Confirm FIS retains full ownership and portability of all data at all times. Describe the mechanism for full data extraction upon contract exit.

Genpact recommends transferring the data back to the client before deletion as part of the contract-termination process.

Data deletion (including backups) is governed by the Data Retention and Disposal Policy and is executed after approval from authorized signatories, with disposal logs maintained.

Exit assistance is coordinated with Legal by confirming Exit Assistance provisions in the MSA/SOW, discussing and drafting the required Change Order documentation, planning drafting/review/approval/sign-off timelines, reviewing changes post-disengagement, obtaining internal approvals, and creating the Termination Process flow document with cross-functional inputs.
Confirm logical and/or physical segregation of cardholder data across different FIS clients.
Genpact intends to be compliant with all the protocols that FIS/TSYS require as part of service delivery. We will work with you to determine the specifics.
Describe security controls for remote/WFH agents handling cardholder data (screen capture, device management, network security, print restrictions).

Screen capture controls: Screen printing is blocked, and screen capture is disabled for remote/WFH operations.

Device management and endpoint controls: Mobile access to corporate networks, data, applications, and systems follows documented policies, with Mobile Device Management and Data Leak Prevention controls. Endpoints use encrypted laptop drives, USB ports disabled by default (enabled only with business justification and approval), DLP monitoring for storage-media events, and mobile restrictions that block copy-paste and discourage saving data to local phone storage.

Network security and remote access controls: Remote access uses enhanced VPN, firewall controls, and DLP protections for WFH. Network entry points are protected by IDS/IPS and stateful firewalls, and access is allowed only from PCI-certified, authorized devices.

Print restrictions and data handling: Printing is restricted to protect data confidentiality. Only client VDI access is enabled from the endpoint, and client data is not expected to be stored in Genpact’s environment; access occurs within a client-managed VDI.

Cardholder-data protection: Card data in Genpact’s custody is encrypted per business, regulatory, and contractual requirements.
Who bears liability for regulatory fines resulting from vendor non-compliance? Describe indemnity and remediation framework for client disputes arising from vendor error.
All matters relating to regulatory fines and indemnification shall be governed by and subject to the terms and conditions of the existing Master Services Agreement between the parties.
CFPB — describe your compliance program and oversight model for collections, disputes, and customer service operations.
Genpact is a first party provider and relies on clients to have controls to adhere to CFPB rules such as Reg F for collections.
FDCPA / TCPA — describe compliance approach for collections operations in the United States.

As a first party provider, Genpact work off client systems and platforms and operates within the guidelines of client SOP's and rely on the client to have controls around outbound campaigns that are aligned with the TCPA.  We can work with clients to help build systemic controls for these regulations.  On the backend, Genpact maintains an internal QA team that reviews and scores calls on compliance related items such as updating DNC and C&D correctly and reading disclosure as appropriate and correctly.  New hire training covers both FDCPA and TCPA and we conduct role plays and/or simulations where collectors have to practice skills and compliance tasks.
FCA regulated activities — describe your compliance approach for UK-regulated cardholder servicing including complaint handling.

Genpact does not hold the relevant permissions or licenses for cardholder servicing. Based on our preliminary analysis, the services to be outsourced to Genpact do not constitute payment services and does not require Genpact to hold payment services or other financial services authorizations. In this kind of scenarios, Genpact assesses and complies with laws applicable to it in the operation and management of its business. With respect to our clients’ operations, Genpact relies on the client identifying laws and regulations that apply to the client and the specific services to be performed by Genpact. Any operational requirements necessary for the operations to comply with applicable laws are written into the standard operating procedures (SOPs) approved by the client, and Genpact performs the services to the standards and in the manner set out in those SOPs.  A thorough end-to-end regulatory assessment for each market will be needed and will be conducted jointly by FIS and Genpact, taking into account all details of each process included in the scope.
Germany (BaFin) and Netherlands (AFM/DNB) — describe relevant considerations for Hamburg and Barneveld operations.

Based on our preliminary analysis, the services to be outsourced to Genpact do not constitute payment services under PSD2 and does not require Genpact to hold payment services or other financial services authorizations. In this kind of scenarios, Genpact assesses and complies with laws applicable to it in the operation and management of its business. With respect to our clients’ operations, Genpact relies on the client identifying laws and regulations that apply to the client and the specific services to be performed by Genpact. Any operational requirements necessary for the operations to comply with applicable laws are written into the standard operating procedures (SOPs) approved by the client, and Genpact performs the services to the standards and in the manner set out in those SOPs.  A thorough end-to-end regulatory assessment for each market will be needed and will be conducted jointly by FIS and Genpact, taking into account all details of each process included in the scope.
Add any further regulatory requirements, jurisdictional constraints, or compliance considerations relevant to your proposed delivery model that are not covered above.

In addition to the regulatory requirements outlined above, we recognize that a multi-location delivery model may be subject to evolving regulatory expectations, supervisory guidance, and local compliance obligations. The same can be discussed and agreed mutually with FIS.
